tasks.prompt: Use docker compose to run a single-broker Kafka in KRaft mode locally. Use the docker-compose.yml below, then verify that the container started successfully.
tasks.starter:
# docker-compose.yml contents
services:
  kafka:
    image: apache/kafka:3.7.0
    container_name: kafka
    ports:
      - "9092:9092"
    environment:
      KAFKA_NODE_ID: 1
      KAFKA_PROCESS_ROLES: broker,controller
      KAFKA_LISTENERS: PLAINTEXT://:9092,CONTROLLER://:9093
      KAFKA_ADVERTISED_LISTENERS: PLAINTEXT://localhost:9092
      KAFKA_CONTROLLER_QUORUM_VOTERS: 1@localhost:9093
      KAFKA_CONTROLLER_LISTENER_NAMES: CONTROLLER
      KAFKA_OFFSETS_TOPIC_REPLICATION_FACTOR: 1
      CLUSTER_ID: MkU3OEVBNTcwNTJENDM2Qk

# Save the contents above as docker-compose.yml, then run the command below
docker compose ______

# Check container status
docker ps
tasks.solution:
# Run from the directory containing docker-compose.yml
docker compose up -d

# Check container status (STATUS should be Up)
docker ps

# Example expected output:
# CONTAINER ID   IMAGE                  COMMAND                  CREATED         STATUS         PORTS                    NAMES
# xxxxxxxxxxxx   apache/kafka:3.7.0     ...                      10 seconds ago  Up 9 seconds   0.0.0.0:9092->9092/tcp   kafka
tasks.explanation: docker compose up -d starts the container in the background (-d for detach). KRaft mode allows the broker to also serve as the controller, eliminating the need for ZooKeeper. The KAFKA_PROCESS_ROLES: broker,controller setting reflects this. If docker ps shows the kafka container with STATUS Up and port 9092 mapped, Kafka is running correctly.
hints: Use docker compose up -d to start the container in the background.
Run docker ps and check that the kafka container STATUS is Up. If STATUS shows Exiting, check the logs with docker logs kafka.
To stop the container, use docker compose down.

tasks.prompt: Access the running kafka container's shell, navigate to the /opt/kafka/bin directory, create a 'test-topic' with 1 partition, and verify the result.
tasks.starter:
# Step 1: Access the container shell
docker exec -it kafka ______

# Step 2: Navigate to the kafka bin directory
cd ______

# Step 3: Create the topic
kafka-topics.sh --create \
  --topic test-topic \
  --bootstrap-server ______ \
  --partitions 1 \
  --replication-factor 1

# Step 4: Verify the topic list
kafka-topics.sh --list --bootstrap-server ______
tasks.solution:
# Step 1: Access the container shell
docker exec -it kafka bash

# Step 2: Navigate to the kafka bin directory
cd /opt/kafka/bin

# Step 3: Create the topic
kafka-topics.sh --create \
  --topic test-topic \
  --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 \
  --partitions 1 \
  --replication-factor 1

# Step 4: Verify the topic list (test-topic should appear)
kafka-topics.sh --list --bootstrap-server localhost:9092
tasks.explanation: The command docker exec -it kafka bash opens a bash shell inside the running kafka container. Kafka CLI scripts are located at /opt/kafka/bin inside the container. After navigating there with cd /opt/kafka/bin, you can run kafka-topics.sh. The --list option shows the current topic list, and seeing test-topic in the output confirms a successful creation.
hints: Use docker exec -it kafka bash to open a bash shell inside the container.
Inside the container, you must run cd /opt/kafka/bin before kafka-topics.sh and other scripts will work.
Use --list to verify the created topic name appears in the output. If it does not, check whether the --create command produced any errors.

tasks.prompt:
From the container shell (/opt/kafka/bin), complete an end-to-end message flow using the console producer and console consumer. Use two terminals: publish a message with the producer in one and verify it appears immediately in the other consumer terminal.

1. Terminal A: Enter the kafka container and start the console consumer first (subscribe to test-topic, reading from earliest)
2. Terminal B: Open a new terminal, enter the kafka container, and publish 3 messages with the console producer
3. Verify that Terminal A displays the published messages in real time
tasks.starter:
# Terminal A: Start the consumer first
docker exec -it kafka bash
cd /opt/kafka/bin
kafka-console-consumer.sh \
  --topic ______ \
  --bootstrap-server ______ \
  --from-beginning

# Terminal B: Start the producer (in a new terminal)
docker exec -it kafka bash
cd /opt/kafka/bin
kafka-console-producer.sh \
  --topic ______ \
  --bootstrap-server ______
# Enter messages at the prompt:
# hello kafka
# my first message
# end-to-end works
tasks.solution:
# Terminal A: Start the consumer first
docker exec -it kafka bash
cd /opt/kafka/bin
kafka-console-consumer.sh \
  --topic test-topic \
  --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 \
  --from-beginning

# Terminal B: Start the producer (in a new terminal window)
docker exec -it kafka bash
cd /opt/kafka/bin
kafka-console-producer.sh \
  --topic test-topic \
  --bootstrap-server localhost:9092
# Enter at the prompt:
> hello kafka
> my first message
> end-to-end works
# Exit with Ctrl+C

# All three messages should appear immediately in Terminal A
tasks.explanation: Starting the consumer first and then sending messages with the producer causes the messages to appear in real time on the consumer screen. This is the produce → Kafka stores → consume end-to-end flow. Inside the container, localhost:9092 points to the Kafka broker. Both terminals must connect to the same container using docker exec -it kafka bash, and each must run cd /opt/kafka/bin before the CLI tools are accessible.
hints: Both terminals must connect using docker exec -it kafka bash, then navigate to /opt/kafka/bin.
If the consumer is already running when the producer sends a message, the message appears in the consumer's terminal immediately.
Type a message at the producer prompt (>) and press Enter to publish it. If the same text appears in Terminal A, you have succeeded.
